# Automatic Certificate Management Environment (ACME)

The ACME protocol is an IETF standardized protocol, [RFC 8555](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8555),
that provides the ability for a client to prove control over a domain name and request a leaf certificate
from an ACME CA in an automated way. The protocol defines a few different challenge types that a client
can use to prove control over a domain, the most common being the HTTP-01 challenge along with DNS-01 and TLS-ALPN-01.

Based on the challenge type, the ACME client provisions a specific resource on the domain it
is trying to get a certificate for, such as a specific HTTP endpoint for HTTP-01 or a specific DNS record for DNS-01.
Once the client successfully proves control over the domain, it can request a certificate issuance from the ACME CA.

## Vault PKI ACME Directories

Vault PKI supports the following ACME directories, providing different
restrictions around usage (defaults, a specific issuer and/or a specific
role). To interact with these directories, specify the directory URL in
an ACME client. For example, with the EFF's [CertBot](https://certbot.eff.org/):

```
$ certbot certonly --server https://localhost:8200/v1/pki/acme/directory ...
```

The default directory policy is the value that is specified within the ACME configuration's
`default_directory_policy` field. The Issuer and Role columns represent the respective values
that are used for a given directory path and `default_directory_policy` config.

| Path                                                               | Default Directory Policy   | Issuer                | Role          |
|:-------------------------------------------------------------------|:---------------------------|:----------------------|:--------------|
| `/pki/acme/directory`                                              | `sign-verbatim`            | `default`             | Sign-Verbatim |
| `/pki/acme/directory`                                              | `role:role_ref`            | Specified by the role | `:role_ref`   |
| `/pki/acme/directory`                                              | `external-policy(:policy)` | Specified by CIEPS    | CIEPS         |
| `/pki/issuer/:issuer_ref/acme/directory`                           | `sign-verbatim`            | `:issuer_ref`         | Sign-Verbatim |
| `/pki/issuer/:issuer_ref/acme/directory`                           | `role:role_ref`            | `:issuer_ref`         | `:role_ref`   |
| `/pki/roles/:role/acme/directory`                                  | (any)                      | Specified by the role | `:role`       |
| `/pki/issuer/:issuer_ref/roles/:role/acme/directory`               | (any)                      | `:issuer_ref`         | `:role`       |
| `/pki/external-policy(/:policy)/acme/directory`                    | (any)                      | Specified by CIEPS    | CIEPS         |
| `/pki/issuer/:issuer_ref/external-policy(/:policy)/acme/directory` | (any)                      | Specified by CIEPS    | CIEPS         |

When a role is not explicitly specified, behavior is specified by the
`default_directory_policy` in the [ACME configuration](/vault/api-docs/secret/pki/issuance#set-acme-configuration).
These directories can also be forbidden by setting that policy as `forbid`.  If
the policy is `sign-verbatim` then the client can receive certificates for _any_ identifier
for which the client can prove ownership.  This is similar to using the
[Sign Verbatim](/vault/api-docs/secret/pki#sign-verbatim) endpoint, but with additional verification
that the client has proven ownership (within the ACME protocol) of the
requested certificate identifiers. When `external-policy` is specified as the
default value, the Certificate Issuance External
Policy Service (CIEPS)<EnterpriseAlert inline="true" /> validates and templates
the certificate instead of a role; ACME's challenge
validation is still enforced. An optional policy name can be specified by using
`external-policy:policy`. Roles are not used when CIEPS is used.

## ACME external account bindings

ACME External Account Binding (EAB) Policy can enforce that clients need to
have a valid external account binding to Vault. Before registering a new account,
an authenticated Vault client [fetches a new EAB
token](/vault/api-docs/secret/pki/issuance#get-acme-eab-binding-token). This returns two values: a key identifier
and an HMAC key used by the ACME client to authenticate with EAB. For example:

```
$ vault write -f /pki/acme/new-eab
$ certbot certonly --server https://localhost:8200/v1/pki/acme/directory \
                   --eab-kid <id> --eab-hmac-key <hmac-key>
```

With or without EAB, requests from the ACME client are not authenticated using
traditional Vault authentication, but are instead authenticated through the
ACME protocol. With EAB however, a Vault authenticated client will have to
fetch an EAB token and pass it to the ACME client for use on the initial
registration: this binds the ACME client's registration to an authenticated
Vault endpoint, but not further to the client's entity or other information.

<Note title="Require EAB for public-facing Vault deployments">

    We strongly recommend enabling EAB for public-facing Vault
    deployments. Use the `VAULT_DISABLE_PUBLIC_ACME` environment
    variable to force-enable EAB for all ACME instances.

</Note>

## Enabling ACME support on a Vault PKI mount

To configure an existing PKI mount to serve ACME clients, complete the following configuration steps:

 1. [Updating PKI tunable parameters](#updating-the-pki-mount-tunable-parameters)
 1. [PKI ACME configuration](#pki-acme-configuration)

### Updating the PKI mount tunable parameters

ACME clients expect the following response headers (`allowed_response_headers`)
which you must specify by [mount tuning](/vault/api-docs/system/mounts#tune-mount-configuration)
for them to show up in the PKI ACME server's responses:

 - `Link`
 - `Location`
 - `Replay-Nonce`

On an existing mount, these can be specified by running the following command:

```
$ vault secrets tune
    -allowed-response-headers=Link \
    -allowed-response-headers=Location \
    -allowed-response-headers=Replay-Nonce \
    pki/
```

### PKI ACME configuration

#### Cluster Path Configuration

To use ACME, a [cluster path](/vault/api-docs/secret/pki#set-cluster-configuration) must
be configured for the PKI mount. The cluster path supplies the ACME base URL to
clients from the ACME directory.

```
$ vault write pki/config/cluster path=https://cluster-b.vault.example.com/v1/pki
```

#### ACME Configuration

The ACME configuration specifies the default behavior of the top level ACME directory,
restricts ACME to certain issuers and roles, and defines the External Account Binding (EAB) policy.

You can set a basic ACME configuration with the following command. This is not recommended for production
use as it allows any role and issuer on the mount to be used for ACME issuance and allows any client
to register an account without an EAB token:

```
$ vault write pki/config/acme enabled=true
```

A more secure configuration would be to specify a restrictive EAB policy along with allowed roles:

```
$ vault write pki/config/acme \
    enabled=true \
    eab_policy=always-required \
    allowed_roles="role-acme-a,role-acme-b"
```


## Links

Refer to the [Enable ACME with PKI secrets engine](/vault/tutorials/secrets-management/pki-acme-caddy)
guide for a step-by-step tutorial on enabling ACME support on a PKI secrets engine mount
and using it with the Caddy ACME client.

[Troubleshooting ACME](/vault/docs/secrets/pki/troubleshooting-acme) - A list of
advice for troubleshooting failures with ACME issuance and Vault PKI.

## API

The PKI secrets engine has a full HTTP API. Please see the
[PKI ACME secrets engine API](/vault/api-docs/secret/pki/issuance#acme-certificate-issuance) for more details.
